## Special Issue Information

Dear Colleagues,

A global response has become essential to mitigate climate change by reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, specifically carbon dioxide (CO2), the primary GHG emitted through human activities. Some initiatives to address this environmental issue promote solutions to achieve neutrality in GHG emissions by 2050.

The generation and use of electricity from renewable sources is one of the most important strategies for reducing CO2 emissions in this net zero emissions scenario. This more sustainable alternative is relevant in transport, energy and industry sectors. However, in the transition to a carbon-neutral economy, and even in the new scenario, it is necessary to reduce CO2 emissions from biomass conversion and cement and steel industries.

A key approach to achieve this goal is the development of innovative and efficient catalytic processes to produce fuels and/or chemicals by CO2 reduction, as an alternative to processes based on fossil resources. The catalytic conversion routes to convert CO2 into the final product could be enzymatic, electrocatalytic, photocatalytic (or photoelectrocatalytic), and thermocatalytic.

This Special Issue, entitled “Green Heterogeneous Catalysis for CO2 Reduction”, will present original research articles, reviews, and perspectives, based on both experimental and/or computational studies*,* reflecting the state of the art and future applications in this field, with emphasis on *green* processes that reduce the environmental impact of chemical processes and products, fostering energy efficiency, the use of renewable energy and raw materials, less hazardous synthesis methods and low by-product formation.

# Green catalysts: catalysing sustainability

C&I Issue 11, 2023

Read time: 10 mins

![C and I](/-/media/images/cni/issue-image/2023/11/chemandindustryissue112023cover.ashx?h=962&w=680&hash=6C75EA91F1B98270B3FDCDD975DCE3B3)

###### FEATURE BY LOUD READE | 8 NOVEMBER 2023

#### Fundamental research is helping to drive the development of a new generation of catalysts that should accelerate progress towards global sustainability goals. Lou Reade reports.

Catalysis is a key facilitator of energy efficiency in the chemical industry. By their nature, catalysts boost sustainability by driving chemical reactions at lower temperatures and pressures. However, modern catalyst designs are somewhat limited by our current understanding of their structures and modes of action. Now, many researchers are turning their attention to new and sophisticated techniques to probe the fundamental nature of catalysis, with the goal of gaining a deeper understanding of factors such as active sites, reaction mechanics and real-time behaviour. The hope is that this, in turn, will allow them to design more effective catalysts – and new, improved, materials.

‘Existing models still work for catalysis today, but we need to solve [global sustainability] problems in record time,’ says [Charlotte Vogt, an assistant professor at the Technion Institute in Israel](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=C3Vp3aYAAAAJ&hl=en). Importantly, better catalysts will aid the discovery of materials to address specific sustainability challenges – such as sequestering CO2, remediating pollutants and generating ‘green’ fuels.

Vogt uses a number of spectroscopic techniques, alone or in combination, which she refers to as a ‘flashlight’ to illuminate the ‘hidden’ properties of catalysts. She believes that rational design based on this knowledge will help to design new catalysts ‘at record speed’.

![Charlotte Vogt, winner of the 2023 Beilby Medal and Prize](/-/media/images/cni/issue-image/2023/11/022_catalysis_inline_vogt_beilby.ashx)

One increasingly common technique in catalyst research is operando spectroscopy, in which a reaction – and the accompanying catalyst – is studied under real conditions. This enables researchers to make a closer link between catalytic activity and reaction progress.

‘You can use several spectroscopies together in operando,’ she says. ‘I like to mix them.’ As an example, Vogt has combined techniques such as infrared and X-ray absorption spectroscopy. ‘Their complementary nature means I can study the reaction mechanisms and how the catalyst affects them,’ she says.

This differs from the traditional approach, where catalysts were typically studied before and after a reaction – with no information on the catalyst’s activity as the reaction happens. The most ‘powerful flashlight’ on offer, Vogt says, is synchrotron radiation – involving a high photon flux of X-rays from a particle accelerator. Using this radiation to study catalytic reactions allows researchers to ‘look at the tiny details of a catalyst [such as] minority species’. She cites a past research project, in which 99.9% of the signal was coming from something she didn’t want to study.

![Diamond Light Source](/-/media/images/cni/issue-image/2023/11/022_catalysis_inline_diamond.ashx)

‘Unless you have a very bright flashlight, you won’t see the tiny species,’ she says. ‘Synchrotron radiation helps us look into the darkest corners.’
In practice, it involves building a test cell and setting it up within a ‘hutch’ at a synchrotron site – after first testing the layout in the lab. This set-up is then used to perform operando spectroscopy, which might involve taking constant measurements over the course of two weeks. Vogt first used synchrotron radiation as part of her PhD studies, to prove that a catalyst restructured itself during a reaction.

‘It worked because of the very high time-resolution at the synchrotron,’ she says. ‘This wouldn’t have worked in the lab.’

Vogt and her team are addressing decarbonisation – via catalysis – in a number of ways, including one rather unusual approach: focusing on carbon in the sea, rather than the atmosphere.

‘The lowest concentration of carbon is in the air, so direct air capture seems the worst way to decarbonise’ she says.

Direct air capture (DAC) – essentially sucking carbon dioxide out of the atmosphere – is energy-intensive and expensive because CO2 concentration in air is only around 400ppm.

Instead, Vogt’s approach is to focus on the sea, which, she says, contains 95% of the Earth’s carbon. The research is the subject of a forthcoming paper and patent application, so she is reticent to reveal too many details. However, the idea is to take seawater, such as from a desalination plant, and use electrochemistry to precipitate calcium carbonate.

‘To me, the ocean seems like an incredible opportunity for carbon capture,’ Vogt says.

The carbon concentration in seawater is 140 times that of the atmosphere, meaning that equivalent carbon could be sequestered using much lower volumes.

#### Double function

![Melis Duyar, University of Surrey](/-/media/images/cni/issue-image/2023/11/022_catalysis_inline_melis.ashx)

Vogt is not the only researcher looking to overturn traditional approaches. At [Surrey University, UK, Melis Duyar](https://www.surrey.ac.uk/people/melis-s-duyar) and her team are developing ‘dual function’ materials (DFMs) – that do two things at once: they capture CO2 from flue gases, then catalyse the captured CO2 into valuable fuels or chemicals. By varying reaction conditions, such as temperature, the materials can be ‘switched’ to catalyse several important industrial reactions.

‘By catalysing the [further reactions of the] CO2 that we’ve captured, we can do carbon capture and utilisation (CCU) using the same material,’ says Duyar, Senior Lecturer in the school of chemistry and chemical engineering.

Capturing CO2 from waste gas streams such as turbine exhaust or cement kiln off-gas is more efficient than extracting it from the atmosphere, she says, because once again the carbon concentration is much higher. That makes it cheaper and less energy intensive than a method such as DAC.

![Flue2chem](/-/media/images/news/2023/1/flue2chem_news_hero.ashx)

A DFM typically comprises an alkali metal oxide as the adsorbent and a nickel/ruthenium alloy as the catalyst – supported on cerium oxide/aluminium oxide. Nickel is relatively cheap, while ruthenium is rare and expensive, as Duyar acknowledges.

‘The ruthenium currently accounts for around 1% of the catalyst,’ she says. ‘I think we could reduce it further – or even eliminate it.’

Once a DFM has captured CO2, it can catalyse one of three reactions, by altering reaction conditions and the co-reactants. The three reactions are methanation (converting CO2 and hydrogen to methane); dry reforming of methane (DRM), which converts CO2 and methane to hydrogen and carbon monoxide; and reverse water gas shift (RWGS) – turning CO2 and hydrogen into carbon monoxide and water. Each of these reactions is industrially important. Methane can be stored as a fuel, for instance, while a ‘syngas’ mix of carbon monoxide and water is a starting point for many industrial chemicals. The DRM reaction could act as a ‘green’ source of hydrogen.

Methanation takes place at 350°C, while both the RWGS and DRM reactions happen at 650°C, with a different co-reactant in each case. These kinds of ‘switchable’ DFMs could be used in future CCU plants – responding to fluctuations in reactant supply or product demand by switching to the most appropriate reaction, Duyar says. One critical aspect of the DFMs is they can drive the three reactions at constant temperature – and remove the need to provide external heat.

![Carbon capture illustration (stickman catches fumes in a net)](/-/media/images/cni/issue-image/2023/11/022_catalysis_inline_co2.ashx)

![Carbon capture illustration (stickman catches fumes in a net)](/-/media/images/cni/issue-image/2023/11/022_catalysis_inline_co2.ashx)

‘Normally, when you capture CO2, the energy-intensive step is to get it off the surface,’ she says. In this case, the heat generated by the chemical reaction provides the heat to desorb the CO2 and transfer it to the catalyst. In effect, an exothermic process is balancing an endothermic one. ‘It means the temperature stays constant and we can run the reaction isothermally.’

The advantage goes further, according to Duyar. By removing heat from the methanation reaction, the DFM helps to make it more selective as it suppresses side reactions (*J. Mater. Chem. A*; doi: [10.1039/d3ta01892j](https://doi.org/10.1039/d3ta01892j)). Duyar presented the work at [a recent SCI conference, Catalysis for a Sustainable Future](/events/materials-ecc/2023/catalysis-for-a-sustainable-future). The team has used operando spectroscopy to study the catalyst surface to improve its future efficiency. There is some evidence that DFMs can act as passive adsorbents – taking CO2 directly from the atmosphere – but Duyar says the speed of this is not known because it was adsorbed by a sample left on the benchtop.

‘When you capture CO2 under controlled conditions [[such as with flue gases](/news/2023/1/flue2chem-sci-unilever-and-13-partners-launch-net-zero-collaboration-project)] you know how quickly it happens,’ says Duyar.

The materials have been tested in ‘proof of concept’ experiments at lab scale and two patent applications are pending for DFM processes. Now the team is working to demonstrate feasibility under increasingly ‘real’ conditions and trying to find dedicated partners, with the eventual aim of licensing the technology.

‘My philosophy is that all the chemicals we now make from fossil feedstocks should be made from CO2,’ Duyar says. ‘DFMs have an important role to play.’

Many other research teams are developing catalysts for sustainability, whose functions include producing ammonia for fertilisers using electrochemical hydrogenation, rather than the Haber process; generating hydrogen more efficiently, by improving the performance of iridium oxide catalysts; and determining the performance of ozonation catalysts, which remove organics from water, through the use of artificial intelligence.

Researchers agree that a broad approach is needed to solve the diverse challenges of sustainability. As Charlotte Vogt says: ‘It’s not just one catalyst that will solve everything.’

Highlights
----------

*   •The review focus on searching for alternative, effective, fast and economical routes for the reduction of CO 2 into green fuels with an emphasis on the associated chemical pathways. 
*   •This review focus on the utilization of various types of bio-catalysts for the reduction of CO 2 into various hydrocarbon products. 
*   •Synthesis processes/mechanisms of naturally and biologically inspired synthetic enzyme- based catalyst with emphasis on the nano-engineering perspectives or the selection criteria of process parameters that can lead to enzymatic approach with well correlated structures, properties and chemical functionalities. 
*   •Existing challenges in this domain and the future perspectives. 

Abstract
--------

Exhaustion of hydrocarbon deposits and increasing the level of CO 2 in the atmosphere is a very perturbing problem. Due to the greenhouse effect and pollution, the level of CO 2 increases rapidly. CO 2 considered as a greenhouse gas as well as basic raw materials for carbon resources. Therefore, CO 2 reduction and its utilization is a current topic of research. One of the best utilization methods of CO 2 is to convert into various products and hydrocarbons _via_ several techniques such as thermal, photochemical, electrochemical and enzymatic. In this review, we summarize the abstraction and working of CO 2 reduction into hydrocarbons fuels using the enzymatic approach. Proteins and microorganism based enzymes have been studied widely for the CO 2 reduction _via_ the redox reaction. The enzymatic approach is a green, less toxic and efficient way to reduce CO 2 into useful products emphasizing on Co-factor, single enzyme and multi-enzyme system for CO 2 reduction to various products and hydrocarbon fuels. Mechanism of enzymatic system integrated with polymer, transition metal and semiconductor (i.e. Nano-hybrids) and also enzyme immobilization also plays an important role for CO 2 reduction application were reviewed and discussed in detail. In this review, we also discuss the various types of enzyme system integrated with electro/photo catalyst and the implementation of the enzyme based catalysts in the semi-artificial photosynthesis system and mimic natural photosystem II. Finally, the new challenges and the novel approaches of CO 2 reduction _via_ the enzymatic catalyst are discussed.

Introduction
------------

Climate variation is a problem and worldwide subject for our environment and seeking attention towards the strategy makers, environmental-related academic researchers and for other bodies. Climate change creates problems for our environment such as global warming, environmental fracas, and social, financial and technological complications. Due to the several issues, greenhouse gases emission considered as the wide-ranging subject for the world [1]. Currently, greenhouse gas reduction is a substantial topic for a worldwide academic researcher.

There are six main types of greenhouse gas which gave directly impact on the environment such as CO 2 (Carbon dioxide), CH 4 (Methane), N 2 O (Nitrous Oxide), HFCs (Hydrofluorocarbons), PFCs (Perfluorocarbons) and SF 6 (Sulphur hexafluoride). Among all of them, carbon dioxide plays a vital role in climate change and environmental pollution [2]. For example, from 1990 to 2014, CO 2 emission is increased by approximately 60–70 % [3].

The global greenhouse gas (GHG) emission in the year of 2010 reached around 50 billion tons of CO 2- equivalents. It is predicted that the global population will be around 9 billion and by 2020 the demand of energy will increase gradually [4]. Carbon dioxide is the largest component of anthropogenic GHG emission. The government policymaker introducing limits on CO 2 and this led to face a problem for the industrial sector to control the CO 2 emission [5]. Throughout the world, the stationary sources are emitting almost 60 % CO 2 in the environment of the total fossil fuel equivalent to 23 Gt CO 2 per year [6].

The Intergovernmental Panel on climate change (IPCC) gives a guideline to all developing countries and industrial countries to reduce the CO 2 emission in the environment [6]. As per IPCC guideline, the target reduction for developing countries is 30 % [7] and 80 % for industrial countries [5] by 2050 to avoid the adverse effect on climate. The scientists across the globe have agreed that CO 2 emission gave many dangerous effects on the environment and weather such as monsoon disturbance and climate change [8]. The European Union set the reduction target at 20 % by 2020 and will be increased by around 80 % by 2050. Similarly, the US also adopts the reduction target of 80 % by 2050 [9]. The CO 2 emission level in the atmosphere has increased from 280 to 417.07 ppm currently as observed by Mauna Loa Observatory (HI, USA), [10]. The leading carbon emitter’s countries of the world are China, United States, Russia, India, the European Union and Japan [11,12].

The atmospheric concentrations of CO 2 are measured at the Mauna Loa Observatory (HI, USA) are exemplified in Fig. 1(a) and (b).

The first research paper was reported on carbon emission in 1981 [13]. Afterwards, it became a very concern topic throughout the world due to continuous increase of global warming. Drastically change in environment or climate change is another reason for the consideration of worldwide researchers and lawmakers for this efflux in carbon emission exploration. Allen et al. [14] review a study related to anthropogenic emission as the main reason for carbon emission and increases the globe temperature by about two degrees Celsius. West et al. [15] studied the carbon emission emits from agricultural waste. They also evaluate the impact that gives by the United States agriculture sector on carbon emission. They also explore the methods which help to reduce global carbon emission. Zhang et al. [16] studied the carbon emission rate in China from 1960 to 2007. Similarly, Soytas et al. [17] reviewed the relationship of energy consumption, income and carbon emission of the United States. Both authors investigated the effect of global carbon emission on their economy and took suitable action to consider.

Ibrahim et al. [18] gave attention to carbon emission and its reduction from the supply chain system. Supply chain management is a part of operation management in which all the negative impact on the environment is included such as emission, pollution and hazardous effect on the society. They discussed 30 research articles related to carbon emission and its reduction. They also explain the difficulties and performance indicators for reducing the carbon emission from each paper. Quéré et al. [19] accumulate the government policies and activity data to reduce the carbon emission in the COVID pandemic situation in the year 2020. The carbon emission is reduced by about −4 % to −7 % during the current pandemic situation.

Carbon emission and the reduction of CO 2 emission is a leading problem to solve in the environment.There are lots of sources which is responsible for CO 2 emission, not only industries but biological metabolism is also responsible for the CO 2 emission. Fig. 2 shows the carbon flux that is responsible for carbon emissions [20].

As shown in the above graph, it is necessary to reduce the CO 2 quantity present in the troposphere. Therefore, the academician and scientist introduces the CO 2 recycling concept to reduce the global greenhouse gas. CO 2 recyling introduces the many technologies to reduce the CO 2 from enviroment such as Carbon capture and storage (CCS) and carbon capture and recycle (CCR). The CCS technology is not economically favourable. On the other hand, CCR technology is cost-effective because it avoids the transportation cost for CO 2 storage. After the step of capture, it directly reduced into other valuable products [21].

In details, there are two types of approach to reduce the CO 2 emission i) Carbon capture and sequestration/storage (CCS), in this process, CO 2 stockpiled in deep rock cracks under ocean and land which does not utilize for the public purpose [22], and ii) Carbon Capture and Utilization/recycle (CCU). In the CCU approach, CO 2 is a capture from the atmosphere and reduced in various products. It can also be a substitute for fossils fuels [23,24]. Fig. 3 shows the archetypal examples of CO 2 capture, utilization and reduction technologies in the presence of the catalyst and split into two compounds such as i) C1 compound such as Carbon monoxide, formate, formaldehyde and methanol [25,26], and ii) catalytic organic synthesis using CO 2 as a starting material for carbon [27,28]. In the process of CO 2 reduction into artificials fuels such as hydrocarbons needs a high energy to initiate the reactions [29].

Carbon dioxide is a highly stable and non-polar molecule containing two double bonds with 1.17A° bond length between carbon and oxygen. CO 2 consist carbon atom in +4 oxidation state [30]. There are two types of CO 2 reduction reaction such as i) Single-electron reduction reaction and ii) Proton-coupled multi-electron reduction reaction.

CO 2 is thermodynamically highly stable molecules but mass transport and reaction kinetics are slow [31]. Due to this reason, a single electron reduction reaction needs high negative energy to activate the reduction reaction i.e. E° = −1.90 V [32]. During reduction of CO 2 to the various product, there are several steps such as electron and proton generate and transfer throughout the reaction, which means need more negative potential as compared to the theoretical voltage that is shown in Table 1 to activate the reduction reaction [[33], [34], [35]]. These overpotential could be disadvantageous because it decreases the efficiency and selectivity [35].

These overpotentials are introducing due to the various reasons such as resistance that generate in the system, slow kinetics mechanism and the mass transport limitation of the reactant molecules [39]. The over potential can be classified on three circumstances 1) Ohmic overpotential (η ohmic); 2) Activation overpotential (η activation) and; 3) Concentration Overpotential (η concentration) [34,40] as depicted in Fig. 4.

The ohmic overpotential (η ohmic) introduced due to the collision of the electron which is moving throughout the electrolyte and the electrode [41]. This overpotential depends on the types of primitive one element such as electrolyte and electrode. The activation overpotential (η activation) defines as the vital energy which directly takes part to activate the reaction and overcome the energy barrier to stabilize the reaction occurs at the electrode at a proper amount. The η activation depends on the type and excitability of catalyst takes part in the adsorption and desorption of products. The Last one, the concentration overpotential is arising due to the mass transport limitations such as the intrinsic diffusion of CO 2 rate [34,37,41,42].

Several Techno-economic analyses (TEAs) have been using to determine the economic stability of CO 2- reduced products over ten years. In this section, we evaluate the product performance from the marketing cost point of view [43]. Jouny et al. [44] briefly explain the economic stability of CO 2 reduction product in the Global market via the End-of-life net present value (NPVs). NPVs calculated from CO 2 electrolyser system that has various CO 2 reduction productions _i.e._ approximately 100 tons/day. According to Jouny et al. [44], the carbon monoxide and Formic acid is the only financially stable product in the market with NPVs of $ 13.5 million and $ 39.4 million. The products like alcohol such as ethanol n-propanol do not have much economically stable market value but may soon have a better worth in the souk.

Verma et al. [45] purposed a gross margin model to estimate the economic feasibility of the Carbon Monoxide, formaldehyde, methane and ethanol, productions. They also reported that the carbon monoxide and formic acid having a large economic market in future if some problems overcome such as maximum overpotential.

The market value of the CO 2 reduced products is not a profitable market as represented in the Fig. 5. Thus, the operational voltage utilization has to be minimum to increase the economic feasibility of the production of formic acid and carbon monoxide with other products such as methane, ethanol, methanol and ethane. Additionally, the electrochemical system has an anode & cathode catalyst that has a better quality to enhance the performance and production of CO 2 reduction products. Despite this, capital investment and economic feasibility still need more attention and consideration for more practical analysis [38].

To overcome the overpotential is one of the tasks in CO 2 reduction application. Many types of research are focussed on exploring catalyst that reduces the overpotential and increases the product selectivity and conversion efficiency [[46], [47], [48]]. Currently, it has attracted attention for reducing CO 2 even while mimicking artificial photosynthesis [49,50], thermochemical [51,52], photochemical conversion [53,54], and electrochemical techniques [55,56]. All these approaches need electron injection from an energy source such as light energy to activate the reaction. There is another method to provide an electron injection from permanent energy sources such as indirect by driving electrochemical processes or direct by irradiation [57]. To overcome the above problem, bio-inspired materials and bio-based material based on photosynthesis model and other biological approaches gained high interest. These all bio-inspired materials and bio-based materials directly used as a catalyst in photochemical, photo-electrochemical and electrochemical methods [[58], [59], [60]].

Now we gave attention on the biocatalyst such as enzymes and microorganism directly in the above application that is beneficial for the utilization in CO 2 reduction. Biocatalyst is innate enzymes based catalyst and has noteworthy application in the pharmaceuticals and drug intermediates. The main advantage of the biocatalyst is that it provides high chemo-selectivity, enantioselectivity, and regioselectivity [61], high selectivity, and high yield of the product even in mild condition. It can also be used the commercial-scale application of CO 2 reduction [62]. Especially enzymes have a remarkable advantage for this resolution, as they artificial produced and it does not necessitate any special supplements and also do not need any culture to store them like another microorganism. Another disadvantage of microorganism is requiring specific eco-friendly circumstances such as supplements, temperature and pH. Bacteriological CO 2 conversion primarily grounded on the fermentation process that is unfavourable for biofuel production [29]. Therefore, the extraction of enzymes from bacteria would be another option. However, characteristics of enzymes depend on the source of the enzyme or microorganism from which it isolated. For a specific reaction, a specific enzyme is used and a bacterial source has to be chosen correctly [63]. On the other hand, biocatalysts have some disadvantages too such as biocatalyst shows lower activity in aqueous solution, to eliminate this drawbacks by the immobilization of enzymes [64]. There are lots of advantages of immobilization enzymes are stability of catalyst is increases, reusability, and inhibition from contamination so we can use in pharmaceuticals and food industry application [65]. All the above points are useful to help the process cost and quality of the product also increased [66].

Enzyme immobilization has been processing via various techniques. These techniques are adsorption on solid supports [67], a covalent attachment via functional moieties [68], and entrapment of cross-linked enzymes with porous material [69] and encapsulation of enzymes in various kinds of oxides [70]. These techniques enhance the pH and temperature range for enzyme activity, thereby increasing the rate of reaction and yield of products [71]. A covalent attachment technique is one of the promising methods of immobilization. The functional supports such as polymers, porous oxides etc. along with linkers help to improve the activity of enzymes [72]. Currently, research is mainly concentrated in the field of biocatalysts for CO 2 reduction applications (i) the intake of two or more enzymes for better selectivity of products, (ii) better catalytic process and ambient conditions for enzyme immobilization [73].

Dehydrogenases enzyme seeking great attention in the CO 2 reduction application and we can use directly in the reduction reaction [74]. Dehydrogenase enzymes are using for converting CO 2 into various products such as CO, methanol, ethanol and formic acid under ambient condition in aqueous media. In this review, we discussed the CO 2 reduction to different products using different enzymatic and pathways.

Abstract
--------

Electrocatalytic reduction of [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CO 2) is one of the most promising technologies available to mitigate environmental problems introduced by the continuous increase in its concentration that has occurred since the industrial revolution. This technology also is attractive from the viewpoint that it can be based on carbon-neutral energy by converting CO 2 into fuels and value-added chemicals using solar or other forms of renewable energy. [In](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/indium "Learn more about In from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") this review, fundamental concepts related to [electrocatalysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electrocatalysis "Learn more about electrocatalysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the electrical double layer, physical and chemical properties of CO 2, the interaction of CO 2 with electrode surfaces, and the sources of carbon and proton that underpin this technology are provided. Insights into recent mechanisms proposed for the electrocatalytic reduction of CO 2 also are provided along with a survey of progress in the rational design of new [electrocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electrocatalysts "Learn more about electrocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Discussion on achievements in the field is focused on the variation in products formed by different [electrocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalyst "Learn more about electrocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the design strategies that have been introduced to alter the electronic and geometric properties of the electrocatalysts. Finally, recommendations for achieving further advances in electrocatalytic reduction of CO 2 in a systematic manner are presented.

1. Introduction
---------------

[Carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about Carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CO 2) is an important carbon source needed to sustain Life on Earth. Historically, its concentration in the atmosphere has been regulated by photosynthetic organisms and geological phenomena. [In](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/indium "Learn more about In from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") Nature, about 203 gigatons of CO 2 are recycled each year [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib1),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib2)]. Until recently, Nature has been able to maintain a [carbon cycle](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/carbon-cycle "Learn more about carbon cycle from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") based on a delicate balance between the consumption and production when both involve natural processes. However, the consumption of [fossil fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/fossil-fuel "Learn more about fossil fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by human activity is currently producing over 30 additional gigatons of CO 2 per year [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib3)] that cannot be accommodated in the natural carbon cycle. As a result, the CO 2 concentration in the atmosphere has increased dramatically in the last 100 years [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib4)], and the accompanying greenhouse effect has a major impact on climate change [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib5)]. Accordingly, it is vital to develop technologies that minimize CO 2 accumulation in the environment [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib6),[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib7)].

Producing an anthropogenic carbon cycle by converting CO 2 and H 2 O into fuels and chemicals using solar or other forms of renewable energy is an appealing strategy to remove excess CO 2 [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib8),[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib9)]. This strategy not only mitigates energy and environmental issues related to CO 2 emission, but also creates a profitable carbon economy [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib10),[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib11)]. [Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#fig1) illustrates a simplified version of an anthropogenic carbon cycle. [In](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/indium "Learn more about In from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such a system, renewable energy is converted to chemical energy stored in CO 2-derived fuels and chemicals. The key to close this anthropogenic carbon cycle is through the reduction of CO 2 to useful chemicals [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib12)]. Techniques such as biological, thermochemical, photochemical, and electrochemical ones are being widely studied for CO 2 reduction. Among these techniques, the electrochemical conversion of CO 2 has several advantages, including mild reaction conditions, controllable reaction rates and product selectivity through the applied potential, and wide scalability due to modular electrolyzer designs. Electrocatalytic reduction of CO 2 coupled with intermittent [renewable energy sources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/renewable-energy-source "Learn more about renewable energy sources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a promising method for increasing the application of renewable energy in the fuel and chemical industries [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib13)].

2. Fundamentals of electrocatalytic CO 2 reduction
--------------------------------------------------

### 2.1. A brief introduction of the fundamentals of electrocatalysis

Electrocatalysis is a combination of [electrochemistry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrochemistry "Learn more about electrochemistry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and catalysis. Electrochemistry interconverts electrical and chemical energy, while catalysis increases the rate of a chemical reaction. Because [electrocatalysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalysis "Learn more about electrocatalysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") achieves chemical transformation at an electrode surface, it can be regarded as a special form of [heterogeneous catalysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/heterogeneous-catalysis "Learn more about heterogeneous catalysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that involves interaction and electron exchange between reactants and an [electrocatalyst](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalyst "Learn more about electrocatalyst from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the latter often being the electrode or a component of the electrode [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib14)]. An overall electrocatalytic process consists of cathodic and anodic processes occurring in two compartments separated by a membrane to prevent the mixing of the cathodic and anodic reaction products, as illustrated in [Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#fig2)a. During this process, a non-spontaneous [electron transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electron-transport "Learn more about electron transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") reaction is driven by an external power supply, so that the electrical energy is converted to the chemical energy of the reaction products. The cathodic/anodic components of the process involve electron-conducting phases (electrodes) connected by an ion-conducting phase (electrolyte medium). However, as the cathode electrode/electrolyte interface is where electrocatalytic CO 2 reduction reactions take place, a detailed understanding of this interfacial region is needed.

Fig.2. Schematic illustration of (a) a conventional electrocatalytic system and (b) the electrical double layer structure at the cathode, which is of primary concern with respect to CO 2 reduction. IHP and OHP are the inner and outer Helmholtz planes, respectively.

[In](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/indium "Learn more about In from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") electrochemistry related to CO 2 reduction, electrodes are under potentiostatic control and influenced by the negative charge on the surface of the electrode. This results in strong interactions between the ions/solvent molecules in solution and the electrode surface, and the formation of a region called the electrical double layer ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#fig2)b) which consists of an array of charged species and/or oriented dipoles [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib15),[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib16)]. The structure of the interface is typically divided into five distinct regions, namely the electrode surface, the inner Helmholtz plane (IHP), the outer Helmholtz plane (OHP), the diffuse layer, and the bulk solution [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib15)]. The closest layer to the electrode is the IHP, which may contain specifically adsorbed reactants, reaction intermediates, reaction products, solvent molecules, and solvated electrolyte ions. The OHP is comprised primarily of solvated ions with the opposite charge to that of the electrode, which are non-specifically adsorbed and interact with the electrode via long-range [electrostatic forces](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrostatic-force "Learn more about electrostatic forces from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The IHP and OHP form a compact layer. The diffuse layer, which is a three-dimensional region consisting of non-specifically adsorbed ions, extends from the OHP to the bulk solution. The Helmholtz layer and the diffuse layer form the electrical double layer. The total charge density of the electrical double layer, which includes the charges of both the compact and diffuse layers, equals that of the electrode, but with an opposite sign [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib15),[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib16)]. The rates of electrode processes can be affected by the structure of the double layer.

The electrocatalytic CO 2 reduction reaction (CO 2 RR) is an inner-sphere process [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib17)], where the adsorption and bond rearrangement of the CO 2 molecule and reaction intermediates proceed within the IHP [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib18)]. When a CO 2 molecule approaches the electrode surface, interactions with both the electrolyte medium and the electrode come into play. Electrocatalytic processes are therefore influenced by factors such as the potential-dependent structure of the solvent, reaction-induced concentration gradients, potential-dependent behavior of electrolyte ions, strong interactions of electrolyte ions with the electrode surface and adsorbed species and their impact on the double layer structure, reaction activity and product selectivity.

### 2.2. Physical and chemical properties of CO 2

The CO 2 molecule is linear and centrosymmetric. The C![Image 14: double bond](https://sdfestaticassets-us-east-1.sciencedirectassets.com/shared-assets/55/entities/dbnd.gif)O bond length is 116.3 pm, which is markedly shorter than that of a C–O [single bond](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/single-bond "Learn more about single bond from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and also shorter than carbon-oxygen bonds in many other multiply bonded functional groups, such as the C–O bond in carbonyls [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib19)]. Although the C![Image 15: double bond](https://sdfestaticassets-us-east-1.sciencedirectassets.com/shared-assets/55/entities/dbnd.gif)O bonds in CO 2 are polar, the [dipole moment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/dipole-moment "Learn more about dipole moment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the molecule is zero because the two bond dipoles cancel each other due to the linear structure. The carbon center of the CO 2 molecule is electrophilic. The bonding electrons are more closely associated with oxygen than with carbon due to the difference in their electron negativities. CO 2 itself is a highly stable form of carbon with a C=O bond energy of 805 kJ mol-1 [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib20)]. The direct decomposition of CO 2 to CO and O 2 yields a large enthalpy change (Δ _H_ o) of 283.0 kJ mol-1 under standard conditions (1 atm, 298.15 K) [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib21)].(1)CO 2 (g) → CO (g)+1/2 O 2 (g); Δ _H_ o=283.0 kJ mol-1

The standard electrode potentials (_E_ o) for selected CO 2 RR half-reactions are provided in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#tbl1) [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib22),[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib23)]. However, the sluggish kinetics of the CO 2 RR means that a much more negative potential is required to drive the reactions at an appreciable rate than implied by the _E_ o value. Significantly, the [activation energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/reaction-activation-energy "Learn more about activation energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the CO 2 molecule to form the CO 2•−[radical anion](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/radical-anion "Learn more about radical anion from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which is often the first elementary [electron transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electron-transfer "Learn more about electron transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") step in a CO 2 RR, only proceeds at a very negative applied potential (−1.9 V vs. the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE) at a non-catalytic surface) [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib24)]. Accordingly, in the development of electrocatalysts for the CO 2 RR, it is vital to lower the kinetic energy barriers and improve energy efficiency.

### 2.3. Interaction of CO 2 molecules with the electrode surface

Under CO 2 RR conditions, when a CO 2 molecule adsorbs onto an electrode surface, it binds to the surface through [chemisorption](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/chemisorption "Learn more about chemisorption from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which involves electron redistribution and the formation of a chemical bond. Discussion on how CO 2 binds to a catalyst surface is helpful in understanding the [chemisorption](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/chemisorption "Learn more about chemisorption from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") process, which often is the first elementary step of the CO 2 RR. [Fig.3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#fig3)a shows the [molecular orbital](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/molecular-orbital "Learn more about molecular orbital from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") diagram for CO 2. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the [lowest unoccupied molecular orbital](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/lowest-unoccupied-molecular-orbital "Learn more about lowest unoccupied molecular orbital from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (LUMO) of CO 2 are 1π g and 2π u, respectively [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib25)]. Both the relative order as well as the absolute HOMO and LUMO energy levels greatly influence the adsorbate-surface chemistry. Furthermore, chemisorption is highly directional, like all chemical bonds. Consequently, chemisorbed adsorbates (chemisorbates) adhere to specific sites, and this binding interaction strongly depends on their orientation with respect to the surface.

The orientation of the HOMO and LUMO with regard to the surface determines the nature of the bonding. Both 1π g and 2π u orbitals distribute symmetrically along the molecular axis. The fully occupied 1π g orbital strongly interacts with the electronic states of a metallic electrode. Effectively, the electron density of the 1π g orbital is donated to the electrode resulting in new hybrid electronic states. The 2π u orbital accepts electrons from the electrode and forms new hybrid electronic states that are primarily localized around the CO 2 molecule. The chemisorbed CO 2 molecule has a bent configuration [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib27)]. Three possible configuration types for chemisorbed CO 2 molecule on a metal surface have been proposed, as shown in [Fig.3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#fig3)b. However, the C s configuration can be excluded because no asymmetric stretching loss in the specular direction has been observed [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib27)]. The C end down in the C 2v configuration is preferred for the CO and hydrocarbon-producing electrocatalysts, based on the fact that the overlap of both 1π g and 2π u orbitals with the metal states is most favored by a linear geometry, and the carbon center is electrophilic. [In](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/indium "Learn more about In from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") contrast, the O end down C 2v configuration is adopted for formate-producing electrocatalysts. As the 1π g orbital of CO 2 molecule is non-bonding, modification of this orbital does not have a strong influence on the intramolecular C![Image 17: double bond](https://sdfestaticassets-us-east-1.sciencedirectassets.com/shared-assets/55/entities/dbnd.gif)O bonds. However, the 2π u orbital is antibonding, hence, the increased occupation of this orbital leads to a weakening of the C![Image 18: double bond](https://sdfestaticassets-us-east-1.sciencedirectassets.com/shared-assets/55/entities/dbnd.gif)O bonds and thus higher reactivity of the CO 2 molecule [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib28)]. The above overview provides a simplified illustration of how a catalyst can decrease the [activation energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/activation-energy "Learn more about activation energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") barrier for the CO 2 RR. The actual CO 2 RR mechanism is far more complicated, as discussed later.

### 2.4. Performance indicators for electrocatalytic CO 2 reduction systems

Before proceeding to the consideration of actual data obtained experimentally, it is useful to introduce the performance indicators that are widely used to compare CO 2 RR systems. The evaluation of the performance of a CO 2 RR system is much more complicated than the [hydrogen evolution reaction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/hydrogen-evolution "Learn more about hydrogen evolution reaction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (HER) or the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) due to the inherently greater complexity arising from the co-existence of multiple proton-coupled electron transfer reaction pathways that can lead to numerous products. The competing hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) also can consume a considerable amount of the input charge. High selectivity for the desired product is most advantageous to avoid wasting electricity on unwanted products and also to facilitate product separation. Normally, product selectivity is evaluated by measuring the product faradaic efficiency (FE) [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib29)], which is calculated via Equation [(2)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#fd2) where _n_ is the number of electrons transferred per mole of product, _F_ is the Faraday constant, _N_ is the number of moles of the product obtained by electrolysis at a certain potential, and _Q_ is the total charge consumed by the electrolysis.(2)FE=_nFN_/_Q_×100%

# Catalysts for electrochemical CO2 conversion: material sustainability perspective

[*npj Materials Sustainability*](/npjmatsustain)
**volume 3**, Article number: 22 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

### Subjects

## Abstract

The electrochemical reduction of CO2 (eCO2R) presents a promising pathway for addressing climate change by converting CO2 into value-added chemicals and fuels. A crucial aspect of this technology is the choice of catalyst materials, which directly influences the selectivity, stability and sustainability of the process. Here we introduce a streamlined supply risk assessment coupled with life-cycle environmental impact associated with various catalysts used in eCO2R for products, including formate, carbon monoxide, ethylene, and ethanol to provide a well-rounded perspective for catalysts’ sustainability assessment. We compare more than 68 case studies in eCO2R using various metal-based catalysts. Our results show that Bi-based catalysts for formate production have the highest supply risk and environmental burdens, while Sn-based catalysts show overall better durability and much lower sustainability concerns. Copper-based catalysts’ supply risk for ethylene conversion is lower and more concentrated, whereas the supply risk for ethanol conversion is more dispersed. Our findings further confirm that improving catalyst performance—especially the stability—can substantially mitigate both supply risks and environmental impacts. This highlights the urgent need for standardized methodologies to assess catalyst stability and novel strategies to further improve catalyst stability using both material and system approaches. We call for stronger cross-sector collaboration to further integrate criticality and sustainability assessment frameworks with more granular datasets and dynamic spatial and temporal representation, for continuous eco-design improvement of eCO2R catalysts.

## Introduction

The manufacturing industry is heavily dependent on fossil fuels, both as the primary energy source for high-pressure, high-temperature thermochemical reactions and as key reactants. The CO2 emissions arise not only from fuel combustion but also as by-products of chemical reactions[1](#ref-CR1 "Bui, J. C. et al. Continuum Modeling of Porous Electrodes for Electrochemical Synthesis. Chem. Rev. 122, 11022–11084 (2022)."),[2](#ref-CR2 "Andrew, R. M. Global CO2 emissions from cement production (2024)."),[3](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR3 "Ober, J. A. Mineral commodity summaries 2017. 202 (Reston, VA, 2017)."). In 2022, industry sectors contributed up to nearly 38% of global fuel combustion emissions alone, which was approximately 12.9 gigaton of CO2[4](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR4 "Greenhouse Gas Emissions from Energy Data Explorer. 
                  https://www.iea.org/data-and-statistics/data-tools/greenhouse-gas-emissions-from-energy-data-explorer
                  
                 (2024)."). These factors make reducing CO2 emissions in the manufacturing industry particularly challenging.

The electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (eCO2R) offers a promising route to produce low-carbon-emission chemicals using CO2, water, and renewable electricity (Fig. [1a](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#Fig1)). When CO2 is captured directly from the air, eCO2R has the potential to close the carbon loop for fuels and chemicals that release CO2 during their end use. Metal-based catalysts are the primary choice for eCO2R due to their high activity and selectivity, but scaling this technology under decarbonization goals introduces material supply risks. Converting 1 ton of CO2 daily would require 170 g of Cu, meaning over 90 tons annually for recycling 1 Gt of CO2[67](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR67 "Belsa, B. et al. Materials challenges on the path to gigatonne CO2 electrolysis. Nat. Rev. Mater. 9, 535–549 (2024)."). As demand for critical metals like Cu, Ag, and Sn grows, the system becomes increasingly vulnerable to price volatility, supply chain disruptions, and geopolitical risks, all of which could undermine its economic viability and hinder large-scale deployment. These supply challenges are compounded by the environmental burdens associated with metal extraction, highlighting the importance of understanding their life-cycle sustainability. However, despite the widespread use of metal-based catalysts in eCO2R, little is known about their supply risks or the environmental burdens linked to the preference for metal selection. Here we introduce a streamlined supply risk assessment coupled with life-cycle environmental impact associated with various catalysts used in eCO2R for products like formate, carbon monoxide, ethylene, and ethanol to provide a well-rounded perspective for catalysts’ sustainability assessment.

## Material Requirements for Catalysts in Electrochemical CO2 Conversion

### Direct conversion

The direct conversion of CO2 takes place in a single reactor and converts CO2 into desirable products (Fig. [2a](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#Fig2)), such as ethanol, ethylene, and formate[68](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR68 "Gao, G., Obasanjo, C. A., Crane, J. & Dinh, C.-T. Comparative analysis of electrolyzers for electrochemical carbon dioxide conversion. Catal. Today 423 (2023)."). Numerous reactor configurations exist, with the most promising for scale-up being the zero-gap membrane electrode assembly[69](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR69 "Ma, W. et al. Electrocatalytic reduction of CO2 and CO to multi-carbon compounds over Cu-based catalysts. Chem. Soc. Rev. 50, 12897–12914 (2021).") (Fig. [1b](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#Fig1)). Many different types of metals have been employed as catalysts for this reaction. The most promising metal for valuable C2+ products is copper, possibly due to its favourable adsorption energies for both \*CO and \*H[70](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR70 "Nitopi, S. et al. Progress and Perspectives of Electrochemical CO2 Reduction on Copper in Aqueous Electrolyte. Chem. Rev. 119, 7610–7672 (2019)."). Metals that benefit the production of other products, such as formate, have also been determined[70](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR70 "Nitopi, S. et al. Progress and Perspectives of Electrochemical CO2 Reduction on Copper in Aqueous Electrolyte. Chem. Rev. 119, 7610–7672 (2019)."). Polymer binders are often incorporated into the catalyst layer, which is deposited onto a support, to bind the catalyst to the support and to tune the activity and the selectivity of the reaction[71](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR71 "Pham, T. H. M. et al. Enhanced Electrocatalytic CO2 Reduction to C2+ Products by Adjusting the Local Reaction Environment with Polymer Binders. Adv. Energy Mater. 12 (2022).").

When employing an anion exchange membrane, these reactors provide a basic cathodic environment which favours eCO2R[72](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR72 "Salvatore, D. A. et al. W. et al. Exploring CO2 reduction and crossover in membrane electrode assemblies. Nat. Chem. Eng. 1, 340–353 (2024)."). Acidic systems may offer a solution to this issue, but they suffer from their own problems, such as an acidic catholyte environment that favours hydrogen evolution reaction over eCO2R[74](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR74 "Habibzadeh, F. et al. Ion Exchange Membranes in Electrochemical CO2 Reduction Processes. Electrochem. Energy Rev. 6 (2023).").

To be economically viable, these electrolyzers require operation at an industrial scale for tens of thousands of hours[8](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR8 "Kibria, M. G. et al. Electrochemical CO2 Reduction into Chemical Feedstocks: From Mechanistic Electrocatalysis Models to System Design. Adv. Mater. 31, 1807166 (2019)."). However, reported stability performance currently lags and will need to be improved to ensure industrial feasibility. As examples of high product selectivity in the MEA cell configuration, studies have shown Faradaic Efficiencies (FEs) of 52% for ethanol[26](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR26 "Wang, X. et al. Some higher FEs have been reported but in configurations less suited to scale-up, such as 91% for ethanol[29](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR29 "Xu, H. et al. ### Two-step conversion

The reduction of CO2 generates multiple products, which are carbon monoxide, ethylene, ethanol, formate, acetate, methane and propanol. Many of these products share the same reaction pathway[8](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR8 "Kibria, M. G. et al. Electrochemical CO2 Reduction into Chemical Feedstocks: From Mechanistic Electrocatalysis Models to System Design. Adv. Mater. 31, 1807166 (2019)."), among these, carbon monoxide is special because it is the common intermediate for the formation of most C2+ products during electrolysis[75](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR75 "Yoshio Hori, Ryutaro Takahashi, Yuzuru Yoshinami, a. & Murata, A. Electrochemical Reduction of CO at a Copper Electrode. J. Phys. Chem. B (1997)."),[76](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR76 "Hori, Y., Murata, A., Takahashi, R. & Suzuki, S. Electroreduction of carbon monoxide to methane and ethylene at a copper electrode in aqueous solutions at ambient temperature and pressure. J. Am. Chem. Soc. (2002).") (Fig. [2a](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#Fig2)).

In an eCO2R process using an anion exchange membrane, an alkaline environment on the cathode side is ideal. However, a reaction between CO2 and the hydroxide ions formed during the process produces bicarbonate, resulting in reactant loss. This issue can be avoided with electrochemical CO reduction (eCOR), as CO does not react with hydroxide ions. This allows highly alkaline conditions to be maintained without reactant depletion, offering a key advantage for this method.

Despite this, a technical challenge with eCOR is the low solubility of CO in aqueous electrolyte solutions. To overcome this, the catalyst is designed to enable fast CO transport to the catalyst layer, allowing for current densities higher than 200 mA·cm−2 to be achieved[5](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR5 "O’Brien, C. P. et al. CO2 Electrolyzers. Chem. Rev. 124, 3648–3693 (2024)."). Most eCOR studies at high current densities use gas diffusion electrodes with Nafion and other perfluorosulfonic acid ionomers. These materials have hydrophobic backbones that extend the three-phase (gas, liquid, and solid) boundary into the catalyst layer, enhancing the overall transport of CO to the active sites and maintaining high current density throughout the reaction[59](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR59 "Li, J. et al. Constraining CO coverage on copper promotes high-efficiency ethylene electroproduction. Nat. Catal. 2, 1124–1131 (2019)."),[64](/articles/s44296-025-00065-9#ref-CR64 "Ozden, A. et al. enriched_points.expanded_content: Green catalysis offers innovative methods for CO₂ reduction, emphasizing energy-efficient processes and sustainable materials that minimize environmental impact, aligning with global sustainability goals.
Ongoing research is vital to develop advanced catalysts and processes that enhance CO₂ conversion efficiency, reduce costs, and contribute to a sustainable carbon-neutral economy.
Adopting sustainable practices in chemical processes and industrial applications is essential for reducing CO₂ emissions and fostering a circular economy, leveraging renewable resources effectively.
A greener future relies on integrated CO₂ reduction technologies that generate valuable products while mitigating climate change, ensuring environmental preservation and resource sustainability.
